figures.gloss: Born Timothy Drew (1886, North Carolina), he founded the Canaanite Temple in Newark in 1913 (per MSTA tradition) and incorporated the movement in Chicago in 1925, teaching that so-called Negroes are Moorish-Americans — Moslems by heritage, Moabites by blood, Moroccans by nationality. He died on 20 July 1929, weeks after his release during the Claude Greene murder investigation; the cause of death remains contested (illness vs. injuries in custody).
figures.facet: He made nationality itself the sacrament: salvation begins not with creed but with the legal-cosmic act of reclaiming one's true name and nation from the erasures of slavery.
figures.provenance: Holy Koran of the Moorish Science Temple of America (1927); MSTA incorporation records, Cook County (1925); scholarship of Susan Nance and Edward E. Curtis IV on early American Islam.

figures.gloss: The Holy Koran of the Moorish Science Temple of America (1927), its cover bearing a 7 in a circle — a 48-chapter scripture 'divinely prepared' by the Prophet. Roughly half reproduces Levi Dowling's Aquarian Gospel of Jesus the Christ (1908) and the Rosicrucian-published Unto Thee I Grant (AMORC, 1925); the closing chapters, Drew Ali's own, declare the Moabite origin of the Moors and the divine creed of the Moorish-American nation.
figures.facet: It shows scripture assembled as bricolage: existing esoteric texts re-cut and re-authorized around a new revelatory center, the whole made canonical by the Prophet's seal rather than by its sources.
figures.provenance: Holy Koran of the MSTA (1927); textual-dependence analysis in Peter Lamborn Wilson's 'Sacred Drift' (1993) and Susan Nance's studies of the MSTA.

figures.gloss: The teaching that Black Americans are not 'Negro, Black, or Colored' — labels of slavery — but Moorish-Americans, descendants of the ancient Moabites through Morocco. Members carry nationality and identification cards, take the name-suffixes El or Bey, and wear the fez or turban as marks of the reclaimed nation.
figures.facet: It relocates identity from race to nationality: the wound of the slave label is treated juridically, with a card, a name, and a flag, making citizenship itself the arena of redemption.
figures.provenance: Holy Koran of the MSTA chs. 45-48; MSTA nationality-card practice documented in 1928-29 Chicago press and FBI files; Susan Nance, 'Mystery of the Moorish Science Temple' (2002).

figures.gloss: The five grand principles of the Moorish creed, recited as the foundation of the temple and printed on its literature and cards. They define the moral order the reclaimed Moorish nation is to embody within American civic life.
figures.facet: A whole ethics compressed to five civic words — deliberately legible to the state and the neighbor alike, the esoteric nation wearing an exoteric moral face.
figures.provenance: MSTA catechism ('Koran Questions for Moorish Americans') and temple literature; standard in MSTA scholarship.

figures.gloss: After Drew Ali's death in July 1929 the movement split: Charles Kirkman Bey led the incorporated MSTA as Supreme Grand Advisor, while John Givens El's faction taught that the Prophet's spirit had reincarnated in him. W.D. Fard, who surfaced in Detroit in 1930 to found the Nation of Islam, emerged from this same milieu; some Moorish factions claimed him too as Drew Ali returned — a claim that remains contested.
figures.facet: The founder's contested death instantly generated a reincarnation doctrine — succession crisis resolved by making the Prophet himself the office that transmigrates.
figures.provenance: MSTA schism history in FBI files and Chicago press (1929-30); Susan Nance; Edward E. Curtis IV, 'Muslims in America' (2009).

figures.gloss: The 101-question catechism ('Who made you?...') memorized by members, encoding the movement's cosmology, the Prophet's status, and Moorish nationality in question-and-answer form.
figures.facet: It converts abstract cosmology into rote catechesis: repeated question-and-answer turns doctrine into embodied daily practice for the rank-and-file member rather than knowledge reserved for clergy.
figures.provenance: Koran Questions for Moorish Americans (MSTA catechism, in circulation by the late 1920s), cited within MSTA temple literature alongside the Circle Seven Koran; discussed in Susan Nance, 'Mystery of the Moorish Science Temple' (2002).

figures.gloss: Seven brief Acts regulating temple order, marriage, work, and obedience to law, signed by the Prophet; MSTA bodies still recite and print it as founding charter.
figures.facet: It makes the temple itself a legal-sacral polity: obedience to civil law, marriage order, and labor are issued as numbered Acts carrying the same revelatory weight as scripture.
figures.provenance: The Divine Constitution and By-Laws of the Moorish Science Temple of America (issued under Noble Drew Ali, printed and recited in MSTA temples); MSTA governance history in Susan Nance (2002) and Judith Weisenfeld, 'New World A-Coming' (2016).

figures.gloss: The red flag with green five-pointed star (the star for Love, Truth, Peace, Freedom, Justice), held to be the ancient Moorish standard; displayed in temples beside the U.S. flag as the emblem of reclaimed nationality.
figures.facet: The flag literalizes doctrine into cloth: displayed beside the Stars and Stripes in temple halls, it stakes a claim to co-equal, not separatist, national standing within the United States.
figures.provenance: Documented in MSTA temple photographs and 1928-29 Chicago press coverage; Susan Nance, 'Mystery of the Moorish Science Temple' (2002).

edges.note: Parent seam: Fard emerged from the documented Moorish Science milieu of 1929-30, and the early NOI carries Drew Ali's frame (Asiatic identity, Islam as Black nationality) forward; Moorish claims that Fard was Drew Ali reincarnated remain contested.
Same historical person: Noble Drew Ali is carried in the NOI-esoteric shard as an ancestor-figure of the Five-Percent lineage; this shard is his home tradition.
The NOI's Tribe of Shabazz reworks Drew Ali's prior teaching of a lost 'Asiatic' nationality for Black Americans — documented conceptual descent within the same movement family.

Era-kin: two contemporaneous early-20th-century systems re-assigning Black Americans a pre-slavery sacred genealogy — Moabite Moors in one, the tribes of Israel in the other — each replacing imposed racial labels with a covenant nationality.

Documented textual dependence: roughly half the Circle Seven Koran reproduces Levi Dowling's Aquarian Gospel (1908), a New Thought life of Jesus, carrying its Christ-consciousness teaching into Moorish scripture.
Intra-tradition: the Prophet's contested death and the reincarnation-and-schism doctrine it immediately produced.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two fixed, short, recitable creeds adopted at a movement's founding moment to operationalize its core doctrine for ordinary members — the Arya Samaj niyamas (1875/77) and the Moorish Science five principles, era-kin reform movements a generation apart on different continents.
Intra-tradition: the catechism distills and drills the cosmology first laid out in the fuller 1927 scripture.
Intra-tradition: the five principles members recite verbatim are printed and taught via this catechism.
Intra-tradition: issued and signed by the Prophet as revealed law for temple governance.
Intra-tradition: the flag is the material emblem displayed in temples for the reclaimed Moorish nationality doctrine.
Intra-tradition: the star's five points are explicitly held to stand for Love, Truth, Peace, Freedom, and Justice.
His own scripture (home tradition: Moorish Science), the textual root that this shard's Five-Percent/esoteric lineage inherits and re-cites.

Textual fact, not analogy: roughly half the Circle Seven Koran (1927) reproduces Levi Dowling's Aquarian Gospel of Jesus the Christ (1908) verbatim, recasting the same Jesus as an Aquarian adept perfecting Christ-consciousness through travel and initiation, against the Word-made-flesh of the source tradition.

dossier.essence: The Moorish Science Temple of America is an African-American religious movement founded by Noble Drew Ali (born Timothy Drew, 1886), dated by its own tradition to a Canaanite Temple in Newark in 1913 and documented in incorporation records in Chicago in 1925. It teaches that the people called 'Negro, Black, or Colored' are in truth Moorish-Americans — Moslems by ancestral faith, descendants of the ancient Moabites through Morocco — and that reclaiming this nationality is the beginning of salvation. Temples survive today across the United States in several rival lineages descending from the 1929 succession splits.
dossier.cosmos: Its worldview is delivered through the Circle Seven Koran (1927), which fuses New Thought and Rosicrucian material with Drew Ali's own closing chapters: Allah is the one God, the human self is an expression of Allah's thought, and Jesus appears as a teacher of inner divinity in the Aquarian Gospel material the scripture incorporates. The distinctive cosmological claim is genealogical rather than metaphysical — a sacred descent from Moab through the Moorish Empire, broken by slavery's erasure of names and nationality, to be repaired in the present. After Drew Ali's contested death in July 1929, one major faction taught that the Prophet's spirit reincarnates in his successors.
dossier.practice: Members take the name-suffixes El or Bey, carry nationality and identification cards issued by the temple, and wear the fez or turban as marks of the reclaimed nation; the movement flies the Moorish flag alongside an insistence on loyalty to American law. Weekly temple meetings center on the Koran and the catechism, and the creed is compressed into five grand principles — Love, Truth, Peace, Freedom, Justice — printed on the movement's literature and cards. Clean living and economic uplift through Moorish-owned businesses were central to the 1920s program.
dossier.canon: The Holy Koran of the Moorish Science Temple of America (the 'Circle Seven Koran,' 1927), roughly half of it drawn verbatim from Levi Dowling's Aquarian Gospel (1908) and the Rosicrucian tract Unto Thee I Grant, with Drew Ali's own chapters on Moorish origins; supplemented by the catechism 'Koran Questions for Moorish Americans.'
dossier.reflects: The religious impulse turned juridical: identity itself made the sacrament, with salvation enacted through a card, a name, and a nationality reclaimed from erasure.
sources: Susan Nance, 'Mystery of the Moorish Science Temple: Southern Blacks and American Alternative Spirituality in 1920s Chicago' (2002)
Peter Lamborn Wilson, 'Sacred Drift: Essays on the Margins of Islam' (1993)
Arthur Huff Fauset, 'Black Gods of the Metropolis: Negro Religious Cults of the Urban North' (1944)
'The Holy Koran of the Moorish Science Temple of America' (1927)
